body:
The Dark Night of the Soul and Kundalini Syndrome

You're here because you're scared. I know that feeling. It's 3 AM, and you're staring at the ceiling, heart pounding, mind racing, convinced you're losing it. You've probably stumbled across terms like "dark night of the soul" or "kundalini syndrome" and they've either terrified you further or given you a flicker of recognition, a desperate hope that someone, somewhere, understands what the hell is happening to you. I get it. I’ve been there. I’ve lived it. And I'm here to tell you, you're not alone. Not by a long shot.

This isn't some abstract spiritual concept for me. This is my life. It was a brutal, terrifying, isolating period where everything I thought I knew about myself, about reality, about sanity, was torn to shreds. I'm not going to sugarcoat it. It's ugly. It's messy. It feels like death. But here's the thing: it’s also a birth. A violent, agonizing birth, but a birth nonetheless. You're not crazy. You're changing. And that change, while excruciating, can lead to something you can't even imagine right now.

I'm not a doctor. I'm not a therapist. I'm just someone who walked through that fire and came out the other side, scarred but alive. I'm going to tell you what I learned, what I wish someone had told me when I was curled up in a ball, shaking. This isn't medical advice; it's survival advice from someone who's been to hell and back. If you're really struggling, if you feel unsafe, please, please reach out to a professional. But if you're looking for someone who speaks your language, who knows that gnawing terror, then keep reading.

What is This Feeling? The Dark Night, Kundalini, and Spiritual Emergency

You're likely experiencing what's broadly called a "spiritual emergency." It's not a recognized medical diagnosis, which is part of the problem, right? Doctors don't always know what to do with it, and it often gets misdiagnosed as something else entirely. But it's real. It's deeply real. It's when your spiritual, psychological, and even physical systems go into overdrive, trying to process something massive. It's like your entire operating system is being rewritten, and the process is crashing everything.

Let's break down the two main terms you're probably hearing:

  • The Dark Night of the Soul: This term comes from a 16th-century mystic, The Dark Night of the Soul by St. John of the Cross (paid link). It describes a period of spiritual desolation, a feeling of abandonment by God, a deep emptiness and meaninglessness. You feel cut off, lost, purposeless. Your old beliefs, your old identity, they're dissolving. It's terrifying because it feels like you're losing everything that defined you. You might feel a deep spiritual despair, a sense that nothing matters, that you're just drifting in a void. It's not depression in the typical sense, though it can certainly feel like it. It's a spiritual purging, a stripping away of illusions and attachments. It's meant to purify you, to empty you out so something new can enter. But while you're in it, it just feels like pure, unadulterated suffering. Know what I mean?
  • Kundalini Syndrome: This is a more energetic, physiological phenomenon. Kundalini is often described as a dormant spiritual energy at the base of the spine. When it "awakens" - sometimes spontaneously, sometimes through spiritual practices like meditation or yoga - it can ascend through the body, causing a whole host of intense physical, emotional, and psychological symptoms. And I mean intense. I'm talking about burning sensations, electrical currents, spontaneous movements, visions, auditory experiences, extreme emotions, periods of bliss followed by periods of terror, altered states of consciousness. It's like your nervous system is being rewired with a thousand volts. It can feel like you're having a heart attack, a stroke, or a psychotic break. Doctors might run tests, find nothing, and tell you it's anxiety or stress. But you know it's more than that. You feel it in your bones, in your very cells. It's a raw, primal energy that feels both divine and utterly demonic at times. For a good read on this, check out The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) or The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link). They won't make it disappear, but they'll make you feel a little less insane.

They often overlap, these two experiences. The dark night strips away your old self, creating space, and kundalini can then rush in, or perhaps kundalini awakens and forces you into a dark night as it cleanses out all the old stuff. Either way, you're caught in a storm. And it feels like you're the only one in it.

Survival Strategies: What to Do When You're Drowning

Look, there's no magic pill. There's no quick fix. This is a process, and it takes time. But there are things you can do to make it survivable, to make it less terrifying, to help you stay grounded when your world is spinning out of control.

Grounding Yourself in the Chaos

When your internal world is a hurricane, you need anchors. You need to connect to the physical world, to simple, tangible things. This sounds basic, but it's crucial.

  • Eat. Really eat. You might not feel hungry. You might feel nauseous. But your body needs fuel. Stick to simple, nourishing foods. Think soups, plain rice, cooked vegetables. Avoid processed junk, sugar, caffeine, and alcohol. These things will only exacerbate the energetic fluctuations and anxiety. I know, I know, you just want to numb it. Don't. It'll bite you back later.
  • Sleep. Or try to. Sleep might feel impossible. Your mind won't shut off. Your body might feel too energized or too uncomfortable. Do your best. Create a dark, quiet space. Try calming teas - chamomile, valerian. Don't force it, but prioritize rest. Even if it's just lying down with your eyes closed, it helps.
  • Move your body. Gently. Intense exercise might be too much right now. But walking, gentle stretching, slow yoga - these can help move the energy, release tension, and connect you to your physical self. Go outside if you can, even if it's just for five minutes. Feel the sun, the wind. It's a reminder that there's a world beyond your internal torment.
  • Connect to nature. This isn't some woo-woo suggestion. It's vital. Put your bare feet on the earth. Sit by a tree. Watch the clouds. Nature has a way of regulating your system, of reminding you of the larger cycles of life and death, of growth and decay. It helps put your personal hell into a bigger context.
  • Limit sensory input. Turn off the news. Turn off social media. Limit stimulating music. Your nervous system is already overloaded. Give it a break. Create a sanctuary of quiet and calm as much as you can.

Managing the Mental and Emotional Storm

This is where it gets really tricky. Your mind is probably your biggest enemy right now, constantly feeding you fear, doubt, and despair. But you can learn to observe it, rather than be consumed by it.

  • Don't identify with the thoughts. This is probably the hardest one. Your mind is screaming, "You're dying! You're crazy! This will never end!" Recognize these as thoughts, not truths. They're products of a system in distress. You are not your thoughts. You are the awareness observing them. This takes practice, but it's powerful.
  • Find a safe space to express. You need someone to talk to, someone who won't judge you, who won't tell you you're imagining things. This might be a trusted friend, a family member, or a therapist who understands spiritual emergency. It's hard to find them, I know, but seek them out. The book The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) can also help you understand what you're going through and that others have experienced it too.
  • Practice radical acceptance. This doesn't mean you like it. It means you stop fighting it. The more you resist, the more it persists. Say to yourself, "Okay, this is happening. I don't understand it, I don't like it, but I accept that it's here right now." This is a moment-to-moment practice. It's not about giving up; it's about surrendering to the process.
  • Journal. Get it out of your head and onto paper. Don't edit. Don't censor. Just write whatever comes. It's a way to externalize the internal chaos, to see it from a slight distance. It can be incredibly cathartic.
  • Set small, achievable goals. When everything feels overwhelming, just focus on the next five minutes. "I'm going to drink this glass of water." "I'm going to walk to the mailbox." Small victories can add up and give you a sense of agency when you feel powerless.

The Integration: Coming Out the Other Side

You won't be the same person you were before this started. You can't be. This experience at its core changes you. The goal isn't to go back to "normal." The goal is to integrate what you've been through, to make sense of it, and to build a new normal that's more authentic, more aligned with who you truly are becoming.

This integration phase can take years. It's not a switch that flips. It's a slow, often uneven process of making sense of the new energies, the new perceptions, the new way of being in the world. You'll have good days and bad days. You'll still feel lost sometimes. That's okay.

  • Patience is your biggest ally. You can't rush this. Your system is doing what it needs to do. Trust the process, even when you hate it.
  • Find a community. This is so important. Look for online groups, local spiritual communities, or support networks for people who've gone through spiritual emergencies. Finding others who understand can be a lifeline. You're not crazy, and you're not alone.
  • Re-evaluate your life. As you come out of the darkest parts, you'll likely find that your old life doesn't fit anymore. Your job, your relationships, your hobbies - they might feel hollow. This is normal. You've changed. Take time to figure out what truly resonates with the new you. It's a chance to build a life that's deeply meaningful.
  • Learn to manage your energy. If you've experienced kundalini, you'll need to learn how to work with that energy. Grounding practices, energy work, conscious breathwork - these become essential tools for maintaining balance.
  • Practice self-compassion. You've been through something incredibly difficult. Be kind to yourself. You wouldn't yell at someone who just ran a marathon, right? You'd offer them water and rest. Treat yourself with that same gentleness.

The Gift in the Agony

I know it's hard to see it now, through the tears and the terror, but there's a gift in this. There truly is. This process, as brutal as it is, strips away everything that isn't you. It forces you to confront your deepest fears, your attachments, your illusions. It breaks you open.

And when you're broken open, something new can come in. You'll find a depth of compassion you didn't know you had - for yourself, and for others who suffer. You'll develop an inner strength that no external force can shake. You'll connect to a sense of purpose that's far beyond anything you could have manufactured before. You'll see the world with new eyes, feel it with a new heart. It's not an easy path, but it's a path to deep awakening, to a truer, more authentic existence.

So yeah, you're scared. You're hurting. You feel alone. But you're also incredibly strong for walking through this. Hold on. Breathe. Take it one moment at a time. The sun will rise again. And you, my friend, will rise with it, changed, yes, but also more real, more alive, than you've ever been before. Does that land? I hope so. You've got this. We've all got this, one terrifying, beautiful step at a time.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
